compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: AI systems that deploy subliminal techniques beyond a person's consciousness, or purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques, with the objective or effect of materially distorting behaviour and causing significant harm, are prohibited. Contracts for AI services must include representations that the AI system does not employ such prohibited techniques.

AI systems that exploit vulnerabilities of a specific group of persons due to their age, disability, or social or economic situation, with the objective or effect of materially distorting their behaviour and causing significant harm, are prohibited. Contracts must warrant that AI systems are not designed or deployed to exploit such vulnerabilities.

AI systems that evaluate or classify natural persons based on social behaviour or personal characteristics leading to detrimental or unfavourable treatment (social scoring) are prohibited. Contracts involving AI-driven scoring or classification must include specific prohibitions against social scoring applications.

An AI system that is itself a product, or is a safety component of a product, covered by EU harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I, and is required to undergo a third-party conformity assessment, shall be classified as high-risk. Contracts for such AI systems must specify conformity assessment obligations and allocate responsibility for compliance.

AI systems referred to in Annex III (covering biometrics, critical infrastructure, education, employment, essential services, law enforcement, migration, justice) are classified as high-risk. Contracts deploying AI in these domains must clearly identify the high-risk classification and include all corresponding obligations for providers and deployers.

The Commission is empowered to amend Annex III by adding or modifying high-risk AI system use cases, provided the AI systems pose a risk of harm equivalent to or greater than existing listed systems. Contracts should include change-of-law clauses to address potential reclassification of AI systems as high-risk following Annex III amendments.

A risk management system shall be established, implemented, documented, and maintained for high-risk AI systems throughout their lifecycle. The system must identify and analyse known and reasonably foreseeable risks, estimate and evaluate risks, and adopt suitable risk management measures. Contracts must allocate responsibility for maintaining the risk management system between provider and deployer.

High-risk AI systems using techniques involving the training of AI models shall be developed on the basis of training, validation, and testing data sets that meet specified quality criteria. Data governance and management practices must address data collection, design choices, pre-processing, labelling, relevance, representativeness, error correction, and bias detection. Contracts must specify data quality standards, data provenance obligations, and bias mitigation responsibilities.

Technical documentation of a high-risk AI system shall be drawn up before that system is placed on the market or put into service and kept up to date. The documentation must demonstrate compliance with AI Act requirements and provide national competent authorities and notified bodies with the necessary information to assess compliance. Contracts must specify documentation deliverables, maintenance obligations, and access rights.

High-risk AI systems shall technically allow for the automatic recording of events (logs) over the lifetime of the system. Logging capabilities must enable monitoring of operation, traceability of decisions, and identification of risks. Contracts must specify log retention periods, log access rights, log format standards, and data sharing obligations with authorities.

High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way as to ensure that their operation is sufficiently transparent to enable deployers to interpret the system's output and use it appropriately. Instructions for use shall include information about the provider, system characteristics, capabilities, limitations, intended purpose, and human oversight measures. Contracts must specify transparency deliverables and ongoing disclosure obligations.

High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed so that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during their period of use. Human oversight measures shall aim to prevent or minimise risks, including the ability to fully understand the AI system's capacities and limitations, monitor operation, interpret outputs, decide not to use or override outputs, and intervene or interrupt the system. Contracts must allocate human oversight responsibilities and specify required oversight interfaces.

High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed to achieve an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity, and to perform consistently in those respects throughout their lifecycle. Accuracy metrics must be declared and technical measures must address errors, faults, inconsistencies, adversarial attacks, data poisoning, model manipulation, and confidentiality attacks. Contracts must include performance SLAs covering accuracy thresholds, robustness guarantees, and cybersecurity measures.

Deployers of high-risk AI systems shall use such systems in accordance with the instructions of use accompanying the systems. Contracts must obligate deployers to follow provider instructions, and providers must ensure instructions of use are delivered and kept current throughout the contractual relationship.

Deployers shall assign human oversight to natural persons who have the necessary competence, training, and authority, as well as the necessary support. Contracts must define the deployer's obligation to assign competent human oversight personnel and the provider's obligation to deliver adequate training and support materials.

Deployers of high-risk AI systems shall use the information provided under Article 13 to carry out a data protection impact assessment (DPIA) where required under Article 35 of GDPR. Contracts must require providers to supply sufficient information to enable deployers to fulfil their DPIA obligations and must define cooperation duties for DPIA completion.

Providers shall ensure that AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons are designed and developed so that the natural person is informed they are interacting with an AI system, unless this is obvious from the circumstances and context of use. Contracts must include obligations to implement and maintain disclosure mechanisms and define liability for non-disclosure.

Providers of AI systems that generate synthetic audio, image, video, or text content shall ensure outputs are marked in a machine-readable format and detectable as artificially generated or manipulated. Contracts must include obligations for implementing content provenance marking, specify technical standards for watermarking, and allocate liability for unmarked synthetic outputs.

General-purpose AI (GPAI) models, including those integrated into AI systems, must comply with the obligations set out in Article 53. GPAI models with systemic risk must additionally comply with Article 55. Contracts involving GPAI models must clearly identify whether the model is classified as a GPAI model and, if so, whether it presents systemic risk, and allocate compliance obligations accordingly.

Providers of GPAI models shall draw up and keep up to date technical documentation including training and testing processes, evaluation results, and information on compute used for training. They must make available information and documentation to downstream providers to enable their compliance, put in place a policy to comply with EU copyright law, and draw up and make publicly available a sufficiently detailed summary of training data content. Contracts must specify documentation deliverables, downstream information sharing obligations, copyright compliance warranties, and training data transparency requirements.

Providers of GPAI models with systemic risk shall perform model evaluations including adversarial testing, assess and mitigate possible systemic risks, track and report serious incidents to the AI Office, and ensure an adequate level of cybersecurity protection. Contracts must include obligations for adversarial testing schedules, systemic risk mitigation plans, serious incident notification procedures, and cybersecurity baseline requirements.

Providers of GPAI models shall make available to providers of AI systems who intend to integrate the GPAI model into their AI systems the information and documentation necessary to enable those providers to comply with their own obligations under the AI Act. Contracts between GPAI model providers and downstream AI system providers must include specific information sharing obligations, update notification duties, and cooperation clauses for regulatory compliance.

Providers shall establish and document a post-market monitoring system proportionate to the nature of the AI technologies and the risks of the high-risk AI system. The system must actively and systematically collect, document, and analyse relevant data provided by deployers or collected through other sources on the performance of high-risk AI systems throughout their lifetime. Contracts must specify data collection obligations on deployers, reporting channels, feedback mechanisms, and obligations to cooperate with the provider's monitoring system.

The post-market monitoring plan shall be part of the technical documentation and shall include a plan for continuous assessment of compliance with the requirements set out in Chapter III Section 2. Contracts must require both providers and deployers to contribute to the post-market monitoring plan, including by reporting performance degradation, unexpected outputs, misuse attempts, and incidents to the provider within contractually defined timeframes.

Where a distributor, importer, deployer, or other third party is considered a provider under Article 25 (e.g., by placing their name on a high-risk AI system, making a substantial modification, or modifying the intended purpose), the original provider shall cooperate with the new provider and provide necessary information and technical access. Contracts between AI system providers and entities in the value chain must define conditions under which provider obligations transfer, specify cooperation duties, intellectual property access terms, and liability allocation upon reclassification as provider.
